answer:
In Chapter 9 of *Traité de la propriété* Vol. I, Charles Comte argues that the primitive utility and value of land—its worth when left in its uncultivated, natural state—is extremely low, requiring vast expanses to sustain even a single person through hunting and gathering, as the earth provides scant direct resources suitable for human needs without labor.[^1] He contrasts this with the immense value lands acquire in civilized societies, where human industry (cultivation, infrastructure, etc.) and population growth multiply their productivity: for example, a square league of land might support just one savage but up to 1,200 people in a place like France, meaning roughly 11/1,200ths (or ~0.08%) of modern land value stems from its primitive state, with the rest created by man.[^2][^3]

Comte supports this by comparing population densities across regions and eras: in ancient, uncultivated Europe (e.g., Roman-era Gaul or Britain), lands sustained sparse, nomadic groups with minimal output; in contemporary Mexico, a square league might feed as few as 6–7 people in remote areas versus 586 in advanced ones; and among pure savages, it suffices for only one, far less than even the poorest European laborer derives from his trade.[^4][^5] Even in barbarous conditions, "industry" like hunting demands grueling effort for meager yields (e.g., ~550 animals per person annually, across immense territories), underscoring that all value derives from labor, not nature alone—thus, the first cultivators appropriated nothing from others but enriched society.[^6]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/09-de-lutilite-et-de-la-valeur-primitives-des-fonds-d/translation/#p-dfb23f).
[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/09-de-lutilite-et-de-la-valeur-primitives-des-fonds-d/translation/#p-e52e6e).
[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/09-de-lutilite-et-de-la-valeur-primitives-des-fonds-d/translation/#p-506a45).
[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/09-de-lutilite-et-de-la-valeur-primitives-des-fonds-d/translation/#p-0b753c).
[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/09-de-lutilite-et-de-la-valeur-primitives-des-fonds-d/translation/#p-cf2254).
[^6]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/09-de-lutilite-et-de-la-valeur-primitives-des-fonds-d/translation/#p-9fbe21).
